segments.s442-g41: بدان is a demonstrative/adverbial element pointing to the previously mentioned ‘that (world)’; گیتیش is گیتی (world) + the 3rd-person possessive suffix -ش meaning ‘that/its world’. جای ده is a small clause meaning ‘give (him) a place’: جای ‘place’ followed by the imperative ده (from دادن, “give”) — in classical Persian the noun can precede the imperative. در بهشت is a prepositional phrase ‘in Paradise’ specifying the location.
segments.s442-g42: برش ده is an instruction ‘give it/give him’: بر here functions like a reduced form of ‘برای/به’ indicating recipient and ش is the enclitic third‑person object pronoun ‘him/it’; ده is the imperative ‘give’. ز تخمی = ‘from (or on account of) the seed’ where ز is the poetic preposition ‘from’ (source/reason) and تخمی ‘of seed/offspring’. که introduces a relative clause ‘which/that’, ایدر is an archaic/poetic form meaning ‘here’, and بکشت is a past action ‘he planted’ (کشت with the poetic prefix ب‑), so the relative clause modifies تخمی.
segments.s442-g25: کشیدند is a past plural verb 'they pulled/drew' placed first (poetic word order). از جای ناپاک is a source phrase 'from the unclean place' with ناپاک post‑modifying جای. دست is the object 'the hand' — the whole phrase means 'they pulled the hand from the unclean place.'
segments.s442-g26: به ایوانش بردند is a coordinated clause: به + ایوانش (iwan + possessive -sh 'his') marks direction/place and بردند is 'they carried/took (it)'. و joins the next clause; جای نشست reads as 'and (they) sat (it/them) in a place' — نشست here is past 'sat/settled' and جای functions adverbially or as the place where the sitting occurred (poetic ellipsis of the direct object).
segments.s442-g27: به جایی 'to a place' begins the clause; که introduces a relative 'where'. بودیش is an older/poetic past form ('was' / 'where he had been') with the enclitic reference -ish; بنشاختند is a past plural 'they seated/placed (him)' (a poetic/transitive form meaning they made him sit).
segments.s442-g28: ببردند here functions as a past plural 'they brought/took in' (poetic b- prefix). خوان is 'feast/banquet' and خورش 'stew/dish'; ساختند is the past 'they made/prepared' governing خورش as its object. The clauses are coordinated: they brought the feast and prepared his food.
segments.s442-g29: همی before the past خورد gives a continuous/iterative sense 'kept eating'. هرچیز is the object 'everything/anything', تا introduces a limit 'until', گشت is past 'became/turned', and سیر is the predicative noun 'sated/satiated' — together 'he ate everything until he became full.'
segments.s442-g30: فگندند is past plural 'they threw/cast (off)'. پس 'then/after' is temporal. جامه نرم زیر is a compact noun phrase: جامه نرم 'soft garment' with زیر functioning as 'underskirt/under‑garment' (poetic word order places modifier after), so the clause means 'then they cast off the soft undergarment.'
segments.s442-g31: چو = 'when'; باز آمدش is 'returned to him' with آمدش (came to him); هوش 'awareness/wits' is the thing that returned. با marks the interlocutor/companion 'with' and زال is the name; گفت is the past verb 'spoke/said' (poetic ellipsis of the reported speech follows).
segments.s442-g32: که introduces reported content 'that'. گفتار تو = 'your speech/words' (subject). با خرد 'with wisdom' is an accompanying phrase and بود جفت is a predicative structure: بود 'was' and جفت 'a match/pair' — 'your words were matched with wisdom' (i.e. well‑suited to wisdom). Word order is slightly inverted for poetic emphasis.
segments.s442-g9: روانم is a vocative/possessive phrase 'my soul' (روان + -م). The following روان is an exhortation/imperative-like repetition (an emphatic or salutary 'go' or 'depart' in older poetic usage) addressed to that soul. گو is the imperative of گفتن 'say', here functioning as a light verb telling the soul to 'go and speak'; پیلتن is a place-name used without a preposition as the destination. The sequence places the vocative first, then the command(s), which is typical of poetic address.
segments.s442-g10: مگر here introduces a conjectural/conditional nuance ('perhaps' or 'may it be that'); باز is an adverb 'again'; بیند is a 3rd-person present/aorist form of دیدن used to express potential or simple future ('see' / 'may see'); بران is a demonstrative/adverbial 'on/at that' (referring back to something just mentioned); انجمن is the noun 'assembly/meeting'. Word order is adverb + verb + locative phrase + noun, with the modal 'مگر' shading the whole clause as speculative.
segments.s442-g11: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' (here causal/temporal). خوردن is the infinitive/noun 'eating' used as the verbal noun ('from eating'); یکی هفته is a temporal phrase 'one week' placed before the affected noun; تن is the object 'body'; باز داشت is a past tense verbal periphrasis (باز + داشت) meaning 'kept back / refrained' — in classical Persian the light verb داشت combines with a preceding element to form the verbal meaning ('kept/withheld').
segments.s442-g12: که introduces the explanatory/cause clause ('for / because / that'); با here means 'with/regarding' and links جان رستم as the semantic domain (literally 'with the life/soul of Rostam' — i.e. concerning Rostam); به دل is an idiomatic locative 'in (the) heart'; راز داشت is 'had a secret' (راز 'secret' + داشت 'had'). The whole clause explains the reason for the fast/abstinence.
segments.s442-g13: ز is the causal 'from/because of'. ناخوردنش is a nominalized verb + enclitic pronoun: ناخوردن 'not to eat / to refuse to eat' plus -ش 'her', so 'her not-eating' or 'because she did not eat'. چشم تاریک شد is a simple clause 'the eye/eyes became dark/dim' with شد as the past of شدن 'to become'. The participial/nominalized construction before the main clause gives the cause.
segments.s442-g14: تن نازکش is a noun phrase 'her delicate (or pampered) body' (تن 'body' + نازکش 'delicate' + the possessive -ش implicit in the lemma form here). نیز 'also/too' links this clause to the previous one. باریک شد is 'became thin/slender' (باریک 'thin' + شد 'became'). Word order is standard: subject (noun phrase) then predicate.
segments.s442-g15: ز هر سو is a prepositional phrase 'from every side/direction' (ز + هر 'every' + سو 'side/direction'). که رفتی is a relative/subordinate clause 'that you went' (second-person past). پرستنده چند is a noun phrase 'several worshippers / admirers' (پرستنده here 'admirer/devotee' and چند 'a few/several' follows the noun as quantifier). The clause describes people accompanying or following as the subject for the next clause.
segments.s442-g16: همی is an adverbial particle common in classical Persian meaning 'continually/indeed' and often used to emphasize habitual or ongoing action. رفت is the past 'went'. با او 'with him' marks accompaniment. ز بیم گزند is a causal prepositional phrase 'out of fear of harm' (ز 'from/because of' + بیم 'fear' + گزند 'harm'). The clause gives motive for why they accompanied him.
segments.s442-g33: هرانکس (literary fused form of 'هر' + 'انکس') = 'whoever'; که introduces the relative clause. او را ... نیست is a Persian possession/experiential pattern meaning 'he has not / to him there is not': او را marks the person affected and نیست ('is not') negates existence. خور و خواب are two nouns joined by و ('and') meaning 'food/eating and sleep' (a stock phrase for bodily comforts).
segments.s442-g34: غم مرگ is a noun phrase 'grief/sorrow of death' (noun + qualifier). با here expresses comparison/identity ('with' / 'is the same as') and links غم مرگ to جشن و سورش ('celebration and his feast'); سورش contains the enclitic -ش 'his'. یکیست is the literary copula meaning 'is one' / 'is the same'.
segments.s442-g35: برفت is a literary past-tense verb 'he went away/left'. The coordination و ما از پس او رویم places 'we' as the following subject: از پس = 'after', followed by او (the person) and رویم 'we go/follow' (classical present/subjunctive/short-form of رفتن used here as 'we go/follow after him'). The explicit ما emphasizes the group doing the following.
segments.s442-g36: به داد = 'to/for (the) justice' where داد is an abstract noun 'justice' (not the past verb here). جهان‌آفرین is an epithet 'World‑Creator' (God). بگرویم is a classical optative/subjunctive 1st‑person‑plural ('let us turn to / let us resort to / let us appeal to') formed with the prefix ب- plus the verb root (here from گرویدن or related classical use). The whole phrase is an exhortation to seek justice from the Creator.
segments.s442-g37: به درویش داد is a past transitive clause 'he gave to the dervish' (به marks the indirect object). آنچ = 'that which / what' and بودش is the past copula plus enclitic -ش 'it was (to him / his)', so آنچ بودش نهان = 'what had been hidden from him' (نهان = 'hidden'). Word order places the action 'gave' before the relative clause describing the gift.
segments.s442-g38: همی is an iterative/adverbial particle meaning 'continually' or 'used to'. گفت is the past verb 'he said/spoke' (here meaning 'spoke'), followed by با + کردگار جهان 'with the Maker of the world' (با marks accompaniment/agency in speech). The sequence means 'he continually spoke with the Creator (i.e., he addressed God).' Note: some provided noun senses for گفت do not match this verbal use.
segments.s442-g39: که‌ای is a vocative/relative address ('O you who ...'); برتر از = comparative 'higher/superior than'. نام و جایگاه = 'name (reputation) and place (status)' — two things being compared against. وز appears in the manuscript as a linking element here (an archaic/poetic variant related to 'و' = 'and'); the senses provided do not match this conjunctional function, so the precise listed senses do not reflect its use in the line.
segments.s442-g40: روان = 'soul/spirit' (subject); تهمتن is an epithet 'the mighty one' (appositive name/epithet). بشوی is an optative/subjunctive passive‑sense form 'may be washed/cleansed' (classical usage of ب- + verb-form to express wish/result). از گناه = 'from sin' (prepositional phrase indicating source of cleansing). The clause is a wish/prayer for the mighty one's soul to be cleansed of sin.
segments.s442-g1: چنین is an adverb meaning 'thus/so' introducing reported speech. گفت is the past verb 'said' (verb before subject here, a common poetic order); رودابه is the subject (the speaker) following the verb. روزی is an adverbial 'one day' placed after the subject, and به + زال is the prepositional indirect object 'to Zal'.
segments.s442-g2: که introduces a subordinate clause («that / who») giving the content of what was said. از here means 'from/because of' and links to the causes of her lament. داغ و سوگ are two nouns 'burning grief' and 'mourning' joined by و. تهمتن is a proper-name/epithet (Tahmatan). بنال is the finite form of نالیدن (to lament) — in epic usage this present/subjunctive-form reports her lamentation ('lament(ed)' / 'was lamenting').
segments.s442-g3: همانا is a sentential adverb 'verily/indeed' giving emphasis. که links the emphatic clause. تا هست = 'as long as / while ... exists' (تا = 'as long as/until' + هست 'is'). گیتی فروز is a noun + adjective 'the bright/world-illuminating world' (adjective follows the noun in poetic ordering here).
segments.s442-g4: ازین (از + این) is the comparative/ablative sense 'than this / from this' (here introducing the thing compared). تیره‌تر is the comparative adjective 'darker/more ominous.' کس is an indefinite 'anyone/no one' and ندیدست is the perfective negative construction (ندیدن + -ست) meaning 'has not seen' (classical poetic form). روز is the temporal noun 'day/era' placed at the end for emphasis.
segments.s442-g5: بدو is the unstressed indirect-object pronoun/locative 'to her/unto her' placed before the verb. گفت is the past verb 'said'. The proper name زال appears as the subject here (verb–subject order is common in poetry). ای زن کم خرد is a vocative address: ای introduces 'O', زن 'woman', and کم خرد is a compound adjective 'of little wisdom' (= foolish).
segments.s442-g6: This clause contains an exhortation framed with a negative-infinitive phrase: غم ناچریدن — literally '(that) grief not nibble/gnaw' (ناچریدن is the negative infinitive of چریدن 'to graze/nibble', used metaphorically 'do not let grief gnaw at you'). بدین is a prepositional phrase 'by/with this' (ب = by/with + دین/این = 'this') and بگذرد is a present/future 3rd-person verbal form 'it will pass' (ب- prefix + گذرد).
segments.s442-g7: برآشفت is a past intransitive verbal form 'became angry / flared up.' رودابه is the subject. سوگند خورد is an idiomatic collocation: سوگند 'oath' + خورد (light verb) = 'swore an oath' (خورد here is the light verb paired with nouns — not a literal 'ate').
segments.s442-g8: که introduces the content of the oath ('that ...'). هرگز 'never' negates the succeeding verb. نیابد is the negative present/subjunctive form of یافتن 'to find/attain' ('will never find' / 'may never find'). تنم is 'my body' (تن + possessive -م). خواب و خورد are two nouns 'sleep and eating/food' (here خورد functions as a noun meaning sustenance/food rather than the literal verb form).
segments.s442-g17: سر هفته (literally “head of the week”) is a temporal phrase meaning “at the start of the week”; را is the definite/object/topic marker attached for emphasis to that phrase. ز + و here represent the poetic contraction ز او (“from him” / “away from him”) indicating source. خرد is the noun “wisdom/reason,” and دور شد is a past tense compound (دور + شد) meaning “became distant / departed” — i.e. “his wisdom departed from him.” Word order is literary: temporal phrase + emphasis marker, then the source phrase, then the affected noun and verbal predicate.
segments.s442-g18: ز introduces a causal phrase “from / because of”; بیچارگی is the causa­tional noun “misery / helplessness.” ماتمش is ماتم + ـش (the possessive clitic) meaning “his mourning/grief.” سور شد is past tense (سور + شد) meaning “became a سور” — here سور is used as “feast/celebration.” The whole clause says that, because of misery, his mourning changed into a kind of spectacle/feast; note the classical placement of cause first and the use of the clitic -ش to mark the possessor.
segments.s442-g19: بیامد is the simple past ‘he came.’ به بستان marks the destination (‘into the garden’). به هنگام is a fixed temporal phrase ‘at the time of…’ and خواب completes it as ‘at sleep / at bedtime.’ The verb appears first (epic/literary inversion) and the subject is omitted (understood ‘he’).
segments.s442-g20: یکی is an indefinite marker ‘a certain / one.’ مرده modifies ماری to give ‘a dead snake’ (adjective + noun). بدید is past ‘he saw.’ اندر آب is a locative prepositional phrase ‘in the water’ (اندر = in/within). The overall order (indefinite + NP + verb + locative) is typical of classical Persian narrative.
segments.s442-g21: بزد دست is an idiomatic order meaning ‘he struck with his hand’ (verb + instrument); و links the next action. بگرفت is past ‘he seized.’ پیچان is an adjective/participle ‘twisting/winding’ modifying سر. سرش = سر + possessive clitic -ش ‘its/his/her,’ here 'its' referring to the snake. Classical poetry often omits the ezâfe and places the adjective before the noun.
segments.s442-g22: همی خواست is a past progressive/intentive construction ‘he was intending / he wanted (to).’ کز is the fused form of که از ‘that/from’ and introduces the material source: مار سازد خورش = ‘that (he) make(s) a stew from the snake.’ سازد here has an optative/infinitive-like force after خواست (classical pattern: want + present-subjunctive) meaning ‘to make’; خورش is the object ‘stew/dish.’
segments.s442-g23: پرستنده is the subject ‘the worshipper/devotee.’ از دست رودابه is a prepositional phrase ‘from the hand of Rudabeh’; classical Persian often places the possessor after the possessed noun without an ezâfe. مار is the object ‘the snake’ placed before the verb (verb comes in the next segment), a common poetic word order.
segments.s442-g24: ربود is a past singular verb ‘he snatched / took away.’ و links the subsequent clause. گرفتندش is past plural ‘they seized it,’ with the object clitic -ش attached to the verb. اندر کنار is a locative phrase ‘to/at the bank/edge’ (اندر = in/at; کنار = bank/side). Note the shift from singular (ربود) to plural (گرفتند) signals that others then acted after the initial snatching.

sentences.s442-s21: Grant him a place in that world — a place in Paradise; give it to him from the seed he planted here.
sentences.s442-s13: They pulled the hand from the unclean place, carried it to his iwan, and sat it down in a place.
sentences.s442-s14: They set him down in the place where he had been, then brought a feast and prepared his stew.
sentences.s442-s15: He kept eating everything until he became full; then they threw off the soft undergarment.
sentences.s442-s16: When his wits returned, he spoke with Zāl, saying that your speech was a match for wisdom.
sentences.s442-s5: O my soul, go — go and tell Piltan; perhaps you will see that assembly again.
sentences.s442-s6: He kept his body from eating for a week, for he carried a secret about Rostam in his heart.
sentences.s442-s7: Because she would not eat, her eyes grew dim; her delicate body too became thin.
sentences.s442-s8: From every side you went, several admirers followed; they went with him, indeed, out of fear of harm.
sentences.s442-s17: Whoever has no food or sleep, sorrow at death and his feasting are the same.
sentences.s442-s18: He went away, and we followed after him; let us appeal to the justice of the Creator of the world.
sentences.s442-s19: He gave the dervish what had been hidden from him, and he constantly spoke with the Maker of the world.
sentences.s442-s20: O you who are loftier than name and station, may the soul of the mighty be cleansed of sin.
sentences.s442-s1: Thus Rudabeh one day said to Zal that she bewailed Tahmatan's wounds and mourning.
sentences.s442-s2: Truly, so long as the bright world exists, no one has seen a day darker than this.
sentences.s442-s3: To her Zal said, 'O woman of little sense; do not let grief eat at you — this will pass.'
sentences.s442-s4: Rudabeh flew into anger and swore an oath that her body would never find sleep or food.
sentences.s442-s9: At the start of the week his wisdom forsook him; from helplessness his mourning turned into a feast.
sentences.s442-s10: He came into the garden at bedtime; he saw a dead snake in the water.
sentences.s442-s11: He struck (with) his hand and seized its twisting head; he meant to make a stew from the snake.
sentences.s442-s12: The worshipper snatched the snake from Rudabeh’s hand, and they seized it and brought it to the bank.
